And you know, so you do something with Heartland of America, nobody else does it, nobody else could do it. You control 76.4% of every transaction, every building that occurs. And so, but you probably don't go far enough in the description of all of that. I gave you a couple of examples of mine as well. Principle 22 on 143. The principle of proof. And here are some of the key things you can do and need to do a lot of in addition to testimonials. So testimonials are kind of obvious, we all know that. And actually probably everybody in the room does a pretty good job of using them. Here are the other things. One, name dropping and association. You demonstrate to people that you are influential by surrounding yourself with influential people. In the rally business where I worked for nine years, Peter, who nobody would ever accuse of being the most charismatic speaker on the planet, made him, elevated his status dramatically by writing checks and surrounding himself with former presidents and former. And so that rally took on a, I mean the rally business was a new rally business been with us since the 1950s. But never before did the motivational rally draw those kind of crowds, get that kind of press. I actually got fairly positive coverage on 2020 and 60 minutes. People magazine on and on and on, why? Because he surrounded himself with a very clever mix of prominent people. So political leaders, world leaders, athletes, entertainers and I would say that for me the name dropping of the list is probably, has been more valuable than the money directly made from the appearances and the sales made at the events. I mean I've like eaten off of that list a lot. And there's lots of ways to get at it, including getting out the checkbook. And surrounding yourself with those kinds of people. Whether it's one or two or 10. I mean so seminars, tele-seminars, coaching programs, forwards for your books, articles in your newsletters, people on your websites. Pack yourself with people that have great prominence and or great credibility or great celebrity or preferably both. And you immediately start to elevate yourself in status. One interjection there, you know, at one of my meetings, just about twice the size, but what I did is before she came out, I played her being interviewed on Montel Williams. It was a good interview, people watched it for like three minutes. And it was like she just walked out and they were like already practically giving her standing ovation. So if anybody's got a really good piece of publicity like on national interview, have the person play that before you even step. And speak, same thing. Well, or you could open a, see there's lots of places you could use it, right? You could open a tele-seminar with it. You could have five stills out of it turned into print. Most people don't use the same thing in 50, 60 different places either and that's always a mistake. Another thing is who you are taken seriously by, right? And so if an influential person takes you seriously, then you wanna make a big deal out of that. If it's a known person to the audience that you're trying to influence. Cause again, it automatically, that's why you want like, that's why you send copies of books out as gifts to influential people. Just like you just did with yours in an industry so that you might get a nice comment about it from somebody who has influence in that industry that you can now, you know, beat to death. Yep. Another thing I have to say about the press releases is being, you know, as interviewed by Bloomberg and Business Week and so on and so on. So that goes on the website. Yep, yep. Like, oh, Bloomberg. Another variation I've got, every year you did a year at Recap, one of your newsletters you did sort of an end of the year recap. We were actually finishing that up for our December one. We had stuff like, you know, national publications we appeared in this year, 19 and we list them all. And you know, number of presentations, approximate number of the audience, you know, all these. Yeah, you actually saw, I did it at the newsletter a few times, but actually that was also my brochure for several years. Was in the format of a, of a quasi annual report, right? And again, you use whatever you got. So if you were in 212 places this year, you use a map with 212 dots on it, right? If you were in five places, that's not particularly useful, right? If you were on Montell, then you gotta find a way to make a, and that's the only place you were, you gotta find a way to make a big deal out of that, but that doesn't like make everybody immediately think, well, shit, he was only in one place, right? And as things age, you gotta be careful of it. So like when I write copy about Hartoonian, see Paul still has in his copy that he was on the Johnny Carson show. So I change it to the Tonight Show. Now I haven't lied, because it was the Tonight Show when Johnny Carson was on it, but it ain't no good that he was on the Johnny Carson show. Half the people don't even know who the L. Johnny Carson was, and the other half, it, you know, is so old it's got mold on it. But on the Tonight Show, what they think of immediately is Leno. That's what they think of, and so they think of it as current. So by the way, even if you hire speakers, you gotta, when they give you their stuff, you usually gotta pretty carefully go through it and rewrite it all to fit your audience and your purposes, and you know, most of them aren't real good about what they do, so there's work to be done with their copy as well as yours. Stunts, publicity stunts, daring adventures and experiences is another sort of proof. Not anything I've done a lot of, but a lot of people do it and do it well. The Bob Allen challenge, everybody kind of remembers that, in the Get Rich in Real Estate field of, you know, he went into a town and they picked some homeless person off the unemployment line, and the reporter follows him around for three days, and he manages to get the guy to have bought a house with nothing down. That worked for Bob very, very well for almost three years, and then he had some life afterwards because he could talk about what he had done, you know, it has another year or two years after it's happened, at least. So it's not anything that I've made a point of doing, although there's four or five things I could think of that we could do, but in most of you, there's something you could do if you wanted to that is sort of in the stunt category. And the Bob Allen challenge, some version of it exists in most businesses. So that's proof. Number 145 kind of gets us to our speaker thing I was just talking about, and that is principle of relevance. What I've given you here is samples that are all from boot camp marketing, but you should not lose track of the fact that it applies far beyond just selling something. And again, if you pay attention like to the way I would write about somebody in newsletters, you'll see that I sort of contort myself to make them relevant to our audience if they are not automatically and inherently relevant to our audience. And so, and again, if you are using like speakers at events, for example, their stuff is all gonna be generic, whatever they got. So I'll give you a bunch of examples here. This is a page out of, well, these are a couple of pages out of our Dentistry for Diabetics pitch. And there's name dropping in that paragraph. So to connect me to Dentist, there's, in this paragraph, I use Dr. Kotler a little, who's a cosmetic surgeon in Beverly Hills. And the work I did for him was about 20 years ago. And I stopped using him for a long time, I never mentioned him, but I started using him again when he was in Dr. 90210 for two seasons because he became relevant again. So now he has some brief window of usefulness to me again. Because of his television celebrity.
